event: Paleolithic humans inhabit the region, evidenced by Neanderthal remains such as the Teshik-Tash child burial in the southern mountains.
The Bronze Age Bactria-Margiana Archaeological Complex (Oxus civilization) flourishes across the Amu Darya basin with fortified urban centers.
Settled Iranian-speaking peoples establish the oasis cultures of Sogdiana, Bactria, and Khwarezm with irrigated agriculture.
Cyrus the Great incorporates Sogdiana, Bactria, and Khwarezm into the Achaemenid Persian Empire as eastern satrapies.
Alexander the Great conquers Sogdiana and Bactria, founding Hellenistic cities and marrying the Bactrian princess Roxana.
The Greco-Bactrian Kingdom emerges as a wealthy Hellenistic state controlling much of Central Asia.
Nomadic Yuezhi overrun Bactria, and the region becomes a hub on the emerging Silk Road trade network.
The Kushan Empire rises, unifying Bactria and surrounding lands and promoting Buddhism and long-distance Silk Road commerce.
Sogdian merchants dominate trans-Asian trade, with Samarkand and Bukhara becoming prosperous commercial city-states.
The Western Turkic Khaganate extends control over the Sogdian city-states of Transoxiana.
Arab armies under Qutayba ibn Muslim conquer Bukhara and Samarkand, beginning the Islamization of Transoxiana.
The Samanid dynasty rises in Bukhara, ushering in a golden age of Persian-Islamic culture and learning.
The polymath Ibn Sina (Avicenna) is born near Bukhara, later writing landmark works in medicine and philosophy.
The Turkic Karakhanids capture Bukhara, ending Samanid rule and accelerating the Turkicization of the region.
The Khwarezmian dynasty establishes itself, growing into a powerful empire centered on Khwarezm and Transoxiana.
Genghis Khan's Mongols sack Bukhara and Samarkand, devastating the cities and absorbing the region into the Mongol Empire.
Timur (Tamerlane) seizes power and makes Samarkand the capital of a vast empire stretching across Central Asia and beyond.
Timur dies while campaigning toward China, leaving Samarkand adorned with monumental architecture like the Registan.
Timur's grandson Ulugh Beg governs Samarkand, building a famed astronomical observatory and a madrasa for the sciences.
Muhammad Shaybani leads the Uzbek tribes to conquer Transoxiana, founding the Shaybanid dynasty and the Khanate of Bukhara.
The Khanate of Khiva is established in Khwarezm as a separate Uzbek state.
The Janid (Astrakhanid) dynasty assumes rule over the Khanate of Bukhara.
The Khanate of Kokand is founded in the Fergana Valley, becoming the third major Uzbek khanate.
The Manghit dynasty consolidates power in Bukhara, transforming the khanate into the Emirate of Bukhara.
Russian imperial forces capture Tashkent, marking the decisive expansion of Tsarist control into the region.
Russia defeats the Emirate of Bukhara and seizes Samarkand, making the emirate a protectorate.
The Khanate of Khiva becomes a Russian protectorate after a military campaign.
The Khanate of Kokand is abolished and annexed directly into Russian Turkestan.
A widespread revolt against Russian wartime labor conscription is brutally suppressed across Turkestan.
The Turkestan Autonomous Soviet Socialist Republic is proclaimed as Bolshevik power spreads after the Russian Revolution.
The Red Army overthrows the Emirate of Bukhara and the Khanate of Khiva, establishing Soviet people's republics.
The Uzbek Soviet Socialist Republic is created through Soviet national delimitation of Central Asia.
Samarkand serves as the first capital of the Uzbek SSR before the capital moves to Tashkent in 1930.
Tajikistan is detached from the Uzbek SSR and elevated to a separate union republic.
Stalin's Great Purge devastates the Uzbek political and intellectual elite with mass arrests and executions.
Large-scale Soviet cotton monoculture expands, intensifying irrigation diversions from the rivers feeding the Aral Sea.
A massive earthquake devastates Tashkent, prompting a Soviet-wide reconstruction of the city.
Islam Karimov becomes First Secretary of the Communist Party of Uzbekistan amid rising ethnic and economic tensions.
The Uzbek SSR declares sovereignty and Islam Karimov is elected the republic's first president.
Uzbekistan declares independence from the Soviet Union on 31 August following the failed Moscow coup.
Uzbekistan is admitted to the United Nations and adopts a new post-Soviet constitution.
Uzbekistan introduces its national currency, the soum, replacing the Russian ruble.
Uzbekistan grants the United States use of the Karshi-Khanabad air base for operations in Afghanistan.
Government forces violently suppress unrest in Andijan, killing hundreds and drawing international condemnation.
President Islam Karimov dies, and Shavkat Mirziyoyev assumes leadership and launches political and economic liberalization.
Mirziyoyev liberalizes currency controls, floating the soum and opening the economy to foreign investment and tourism.
Voters approve a constitutional referendum extending presidential terms, allowing Mirziyoyev to remain in power.
